coredata.dc:description: Azodicarbonamide (ADA) in flour can be easily decomposed to semi-carbazide and biuret, exhibiting strong genotoxicity in vitro and carcinogenicity. Glutathione (GSH) can be conjugated with some ketone-containing compounds and unsaturated aldehydes to form toxic metabolites. Here, a novel ratio fluorescence probe based on blue emitting biomass-derived carbon dots (BCDs) and yellow emitting 2,3-diaminophenazine (OxOPD) was prepared for the bifunctional determination of glutathione (GSH) and ADA. This strategy includes three processes: (1) Ag+ oxidizes o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce OxOPD. The peak at 562 nm was enhanced, and the peak at 442 nm was reduced due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), (2) glutathione binds Ag+ and inhibits the production of OxOPD, (3) ADA oxidizes GSH to form GSSG, resulting in the release of Ag+ by GSH. Therefore, the newly designed ratio fluorescence probe can be based on the intensity ratio (I442/I562) changes and significant fluorescent color changes to detect GSH and ADA. Moreover, a smartphone WeChat applet and a yolov3-assisted deep learning classification model have been developed to quickly detect GSH and ADA on-site based on an image processing algorithm. These results indicate that smartphone ratiometric fluorescence sensing combined with machine learning has broad prospects for biomedical analysis.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Tao Liu and Suru Chen should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Azodicarbonamide (ADA) in flour can be easily decomposed to semi-carbazide and biuret, exhibiting strong genotoxicity in vitro and carcinogenicity. Glutathione (GSH) can be conjugated with some ketone-containing compounds and unsaturated aldehydes to form toxic metabolites. Here, a novel ratio fluorescence probe based on blue emitting biomass-derived carbon dots (BCDs) and yellow emitting 2,3-diaminophenazine (OxOPD) was prepared for the bifunctional determination of glutathione (GSH) and ADA. This strategy includes three processes: (1) Ag+ oxidizes o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce OxOPD. The peak at 562 nm was enhanced, and the peak at 442 nm was reduced due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), (2) glutathione binds Ag+ and inhibits the production of OxOPD, (3) ADA oxidizes GSH to form GSSG, resulting in the release of Ag+ by GSH. Therefore, the newly designed ratio fluorescence probe can be based on the intensity ratio (I442/I562) changes and significant fluorescent color changes to detect GSH and ADA. Moreover, a smartphone WeChat applet and a yolov3-assisted deep learning classification model have been developed to quickly detect GSH and ADA on-site based on an image processing algorithm. These results indicate that smartphone ratiometric fluorescence sensing combined with machine learning has broad prospects for biomedical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: People pay more and more attention to food safety, and it has gradually become a global problem (Jackson, 2009). Among many factors that cause food safety issues, the illegal addition and abuse of food additives is the main one. Flour-related products account for a large part of the family diet in many countries. The whiteness and gluten of flour can be improved by adding additives. As a food additive, azodicarbonamide (ADA) is widely used in flour products serving as an oxidizing agent or a bleaching agent in some countries (Che et al., 2017; Lopez-Gonzalez et al., 2019). More seriously, when ADA is contacted with wet flour, ADA can be easily decomposed to semi-carbazide and biuret, exhibiting strong genotoxicity in vitro and carcinogenicity (Bechtold et al., 1988; Chen et al., 2018). Therefore, long-term consumption of flour products exceeding ADA will cause serious harm to the human body. Consequently, in some countries such as Singapore, Japan, and Australia, the addition amount of ADA in flour is not allowed to exceed 45 mg/kg (ppm). ADA has even been banned as a food additive in China, Canada, and the United States (Chen et al., 2019). Thus, the detection of ADA content is very important to ensure flour quality and the safety of the human diet.

Glutathione (GSH) can be conjugated with some ketone-containing compounds and unsaturated aldehydes to form toxic metabolites (Monks et al., 1990). GSH is a non-protein sulfhydryl biomolecule, which plays an important part in maintaining the balance of cell redox activity and normal immune system function (Krauth-Siegel et al., 2005). However, abnormal glutathione levels in the human body may lead to a variety of diseases, such as liver injury (Finamor et al., 2017), Alzheimer's disease (Mandal et al., 2015), lung injury (Qian et al., 2018), and other diseases. The current laboratory-based traditional analysis techniques for biomolecules and food additives include enzyme-linked immunoassay (Hall et al., 2021), mass spectrometry (Zhang et al., 2018), electrochemistry (Liu et al., 2013), colorimetric (Chen et al., 2016), and chromatography (Sun et al., 2019). Although these technologies exhibit satisfactory accuracy and resolution for target recognition and analysis, the shortcomings of expensive instruments, complex operation steps, and time-consuming pre-processing seriously restrict their wide applications, especially in the case of on-site monitoring with limited resources. Therefore, it is the key to developing real-time sensing technology for biomolecules and food additives in a portable and rapid manner.

Currently, optical analysis methods have attracted attention due to their simple and intuitive operation and low cost. In particular, the ratio fluorescence sensor has attracted extensive attention because of its advantages of self-calibration and high selectivity (Castro et al., 2019). As a new type of signal reporter with fluorescent properties, carbon dots (CDs) can not only maintain excellent fluorescence performance, but also overcome the shortcomings of poor light stability, high toxicity, and complex preparation processes (Gao et al., 2016; Kaur et al., 2019; Rodrigues et al., 2017). Particularly, “green” carbon dots prepared from renewable resources-biomass are very suitable for large-scale production and show important economic value due to their wide sources, environmental friendliness, and excellent optical properties (D et al., 2019). As a functional monomer and chromogenic substrate, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) has been successfully used in spectral and electrochemical analysis (Wang et al., 2017, 2019; Yu et al., 2015). The introduction of metal ions with oxidation properties into the system to participate in the oxidation reaction of OPD, and together with nanomaterials as a reporting unit, has also been successfully realized, For example, Xu et al. (2019) used Cu2+ to oxidize OPD to detection of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) activity.

Considering that the fluorescence in the pixel of the sample image is the measurement of the sample light, and the qualitative or quantitative determination of the sample is based on the change of some pixel models (Bueno et al., 2016), such as RGB, HSV, CIE, etc. Since RGB and HSV are important indicators of color expression, it has been reported that RGB and HSV values are used as analysis and detection parameters (Bueno et al., 2016; Maruthupandi et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021). Moreover, smartphones have high-resolution cameras and powerful data processing capabilities. Smartphones have been proposed as chemical and biological detection platforms (Kanchi et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2019; Zhang and Liu, 2016). Because of its high efficiency, fast speed, portability, and low cost, it has been widely used (Lin et al., 2018). However, it is difficult to extract useful information from a large number of images using any conventional methods. Algorithms based on deep learning offer a promising solution for changing this situation. Based on these advantages of smartphones, we have developed an applet of WeChat called "intelligent fluorescence analysis", which adopts the yolov3 algorithm of machine learning and processes the obtained image combined with ordinary image analysis. The program has achieved good results in fluorescence image processing. Under the support of 3D printing technology, several robust and portable devices have been printed to suit the construction of the fluorescence sensors integrated with smartphones (Carrasco-Correa et al., 2021). However, those sensing platforms need the computer processing software (MATLAB and Image J) to analyze data, which caused the complexity of images processing and could not meet the needs of real-time monitoring.

Herein, a handheld multifunctional smartphone platform integrated with 3D printing equipment based on ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobes (RFNPs) system was developed for convenient, visual, and on-site/real-time determination of ADA and GSH in sample. In this system, the BCDs and O-phenylenediamine were used as the indicators. Under the stimulus trigger of Ag+, the nonfluorescent OPD was oxidized to produce OxOPD with a strong fluorescence intensity at 562 nm. Meanwhile, blue emissive BCDs served as a responsive unit with low toxicity and high fluorescence intensity were forcefully quenched by OxOPD through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), exhibiting a typical ratiometric fluorescence response. Due to the ion-binding between Ag+ and -SH of GSH, the blue fluorescence of BCDs was enhanced gradually but the fluorescence of OxOPD was reduced, appearing an obvious and wide color variation from orange to blue under UV light. After the addition of ADA, the GSH was oxidized to yield GSSG (glutathione disulfide), which caused GSH to release Ag+. Thus, the fluorescence of BCDs would be quenched again by the addition of ADA, whereas the fluorescence of OxOPD was recovered throughout the process. The detection of ADA and GSH is of great significance in ensuring food safety, protecting human health, and early diagnosis of diseases. To provide an on-site sensing platform, the fluorescence colors on the cuvettes were recorded by the smartphone's built-in cameras, together with the 3D printing portable equipment assistance. The collected pictures are processed by the self-developed WeChat applet. Therefore, the proposed sensing strategy opens up a new way for online, real-time/on-site medical diagnosis and food safety monitoring. This work fills the marriage between smartphone fluorescence sensing technology and powerful deep learning algorithms and demonstrates the key means of real-time visualization of intelligent detection.
Reagents and chemicals, characterization equipment were listed in the Supporting information.

BCDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. Specifically, the fresh orange leaves were washed with deionized water, then dried at 80 ℃ for 10 h, and finally crushed. Next, weigh 0.5 g of orange leaves and add 10 mL deionized water. After then, these above-mixed solutions were transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. When it is cooled to room temperature, it is centrifuged (8000 r/min), the supernatant was placed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 and dialyzed with ultrapure water for 4 days to obtain brown BCDs suspension. Finally, the BCDs suspension is freeze-dried to obtain pure BCDs solid powder, which is stored in a refrigerator at −4 °C for further use.

To detect GSH, 200 µL of OPD (3 mM), 200 µL of Ag+ (1.35 mM), and 200 µL of GSH standard solutions of different concentrations were mixed into a test tube containing 2 mL of PB buffer (10 mM, pH=5.5). 200 µL BCDs (0.1 g/L) and 200 µL of the aqueous solution were added to the above test tube, and the resulting mixed solution was reacted at 25 °C for 20 min. A standard curve was constructed by the ratio of the fluorescence emission intensity (I442/I562) at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm and the concentration of GSH in the solution, and the GSH was quantified by the standard curve and the scanning data of the fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Human serum specimens were provided by Sichuan Agricultural University Hospital. Before analysis, the serum sample was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was collected. 200 µL of the supernatant was added to 200 µL of different standard concentrations of GSH using the standard addition method; then 200 µL of Ag+ (1.35 mM) solution was added to the mix. Finally, 200 µL of OPD (3 mM) and 200 µL of BCDs solution (0.1 g/L) were added to the above-mixed solution. The solution was scanned by a fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the scanning data was substituted into the standard curve constructed by GSH to obtain the GSH concentration in the serum, and the recovery rate was checked. The entire experimental operation was repeated three times.

200 µL of GSH (3 mM) and 200 µL of ADA standard solutions of different concentrations were added to a 2 mL PB buffer (10 mM, pH=5.5) test tube, then 200 µL of OPD (3 mM), 200 µL of Ag+ (1.35 mM) and 200 µL BCDs (0.1 g/L) were added to the above test tube. The resulting mixed solution was reacted at 25 °C for 20 min. The fluorescence emission intensity ratio under excitation at 365 nm wavelength (I442/I562) was constructed as a standard curve with the concentration of ADA in the solution and quantifying GSH was through the above standard curve and the scanning data of a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The actual flour samples were purchased from Sichuan Agricultural Campus Supermarket. Before analysis, 0.1 g flour and 10 mL acetone were mixed and sonicated for 20 min. After centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min to remove insoluble materials, the 5 mL supernatant was transferred to a rotary evaporation flask for rotary evaporation. The evaporated concentrate is re-dissolved in water, and then the insoluble matter is filtered with a 4.5 µm filter. Finally, a flour solution with different levels of ADA added to the measurement standard based on the above method is obtained. The entire experimental operation was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH and ADA based on smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: In order to build a portable smartphone sensing platform, we use a CAD program to design the device model and use 3D printing technology to print the device (Fig. S1). The device material we use is imported photosensitive resin, which is printed by SLA light curing printing technology. The equipment includes an excitation light source of ultraviolet lamp, black box, sample card slot, black box, and mobile phone card slot. Finally, the device is combined with a smartphone to form a smartphone sensing platform.

Place the sample in the sample card slot of the equipment, Place the sample card slot together with the sample in the dark box to ensure a dark environment. Then lighted with an ultraviolet lamp and put the smartphone in the mobile phone card slot and take photos of the sample. In this study, the fluorescence images are captured by the rear camera of the smartphone, and the camera setting parameters are consistent. The resolution of all images is 3648 * 2736 pixels, and the image format is jpg.

From the TEM image, it can be seen that BCDs have nanometer size and good dispersibility ( Fig. 1A). HRTEM image (Fig. 1B) exhibits the crystal characteristics of BCDs and the lattice fringe spacing is 0.21 nm. Fig. 1C shows the AFM characterization data, which confirmed that BCDs are uniformly distributed and the height is about 2.5 nm. From the Raman spectrum, it can be seen that there are two peaks located at 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 and corresponding to the D band and G band (Fig. 1D), respectively. The intensity ratio of IG/ID is 1.24, indicating that BCDs have high crystallinity, which is consistent with the previously reported graphene quantum dots (Rao et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2020).

Besides, the optical properties of BCDs and OxOPD were studied with fluorescence spectrophotometer and ultraviolet spectrophotometer. Under the excitation of 365 nm, BCDs have strong fluorescence emission at 442 nm and an obvious UV absorption peak at 320 nm ( Fig. 2A). Under the excitation of 365 nm, OxOPD has strong fluorescence emission at 562 nm and an obvious UV absorption peak at 425 nm (Fig. 2B). The short-wavelength fluorescence emission provides an effective signal output element for constructing a ratio fluorescence sensing system. To further study the surface state and chemical composition of BCDs, FTIR and XRD were used to further characterize BCDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, The wide absorption band at 3492 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the -OH bond, the absorption peak at 3254 cm−1 is caused by the -NH bond. The absorption peak at 2970 cm−1 is from the -CH3 functional group. The absorption peak at 1630 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the CC/CO bond. The absorption bands at 1400 cm−1 and 1082 cm−1, respectively from the C-C and C-O bonds, respectively. XRD pattern of the BCDs powder shows two diffraction peaks (Fig. 2D), the position at 2θ= 21.7° is due to the formation of disordered carbon phase, and the peak at 2θ= 41° corresponds to the (100) plane of carbon. Moreover, the layer spacing of 0.21 nm is consistent with the HRTEM data.

In addition, XPS was used to determine the surface chemistry of BCDs. Fig. S2A exhibited that there are three main peaks at 533.3 eV, 401.3 eV, and 286.3 eV, corresponding to O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s, respectively. Fig. S2B shows three high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s at 288.2 eV, 286.2 eV and 286.2 eV, respectively. The 284.8 eV peaks originate from the CO, C-O, and C-C/CC bonds, respectively. Fig. S2C shows the high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s, two peaks of 399.9 eV and 401.8 eV are caused by C-N and N-H bonds, respectively. Fig. S2D shows two peaks of 531.4 eV, and 532.8 eV in the O 1 s high-resolution spectrum are derived from O-C, and OC bonds, respectively. The above data proves that BCDs are composed of C, N, and O elements, which are consistent with the above FT-IR characterization data.

The principle of the RFNPs of GSH and ADA is based on deep learning strategies as illustrated in Scheme 1, which mainly consists of five parts. (1) Ag+ as an oxidant can rapidly oxidize OPD to form OxOPD, which has a strong orange fluorescence peak at a wavelength of 562 nm. While OxOPD possessed an absorption band at 420 nm can also be easily observed with the naked eye to be yellow. (2) The generated OxOPD can weaken the fluorescence intensity of BCDs at the emission wavelength of 442 nm due to FRET. (3) GSH can chelate Ag+ and inhibit the oxidation of OPD, accompanied by the change of fluorescence intensity at 562 nm and 442 nm under 365 nm excitation. (4) GSH is oxidized by ADA to produce GSSG, which causes the fluorescence signal to change in the opposite direction. The fluorescence intensity ratio of BCDs/OxOPD (I442/I562) was used to quantitatively analyze GSH and ADA, respectively. (5) Take fluorescent pictures with smartphones, then analyze the collected photos with machine learning WeChat applet, and finally obtain the linear relationship between RGB and HSV values concerning the concentration of the detected substance.

To further prove the feasibility of the constructed GSH and ADA sensing system, a series of control experiments were analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. S3, compared with BCDs solution (a), BCDs/Ag+ (b), BCDs/OPD (c), BCDs/GSH (d), and BCDs/ADA (e), the fluorescence intensity of (e) did not change significantly, while the fluorescence emission peak of BCDs/Ag+/OPD (f) decreased at 442 nm, and a new strong fluorescence peak appeared at 562 nm, indicating that the reaction between Ag+ and OPD produces OxOPD, which can quench the fluorescence intensity of BCDs. When the BCDs/Ag+/OPD solution contains GSH, the fluorescence peak of the BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH solution (g) is in the opposite direction of the signal (f). The changes indicated that GSH can chelate Ag+ and inhibit the production of OxOPD. However, when ADA was added to the BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH solution, the fluorescence signal of the BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH/ADA solution (h) recovered again, indicating that ADA oxidizes GSH to produce GSSG and lose its chelating ability. Therefore, the quantitative analysis of GSH and ADA based on the ratio fluorescence signal constructed by BCDs/ Ag+/OPD is feasible.

To study the interaction between Ag+, OxOPD, and BCDs, and analyze the fluorescence changes before and after adding the detector, we tested their 3D fluorescence spectra, respectively. From the 3D fluorescence diagram of single BCDs ( Fig. 3A) and the 3D fluorescence diagram of carbon point after adding Ag+ (Fig. 3B), OPD (Fig. 3C), GSH (Fig. 3D), and ADA (Fig. 3E), there is no significant change in the fluorescence diagram of BCDs, indicating that Ag+, OPD, GSH, and ADA will not interfere with the fluorescence of BCDs. In the 3D fluorescence spectra of Ag+ and OPD mixtures, as shown in Fig. 3F, a large light spot appears in the excitation wavelength range of 460–370 nm, which proves that Ag+ and OPD can have obvious interactions. However, when Ag+ and OPD were added to BCDs (Fig. 3G), we can see that the fluorescence of BCDs decreased, while the fluorescence of OxOPD at 562 nm did not change significantly. If GSH is in the system, due to the complexation of Ag+ with GSH, the fluorescence emission enhancement of BCDs is at 442 nm, while the fluorescence of OxOPD is reduced (Fig. 3H). If both GSH and ADA are in the system, due to the oxidation of GSH by ADA to form GSSG accompanied by Ag+ release, the fluorescence of OxOPD increased and the fluorescence of BCDs decreased (Fig. 3I). The above results further illustrate the feasibility of the BCDs/Ag+/OPD sensing system.

In order to explore the response mechanism, we tested the UV spectroscopy and found that the UV absorption band of OxOPD overlaps the emission spectrum of BCDs (Fig. S4A), which indicates that the quenching mechanism of BCDs fluorescence may be caused by FRET (Rong et al., 2021) or (IFE) (Ran et al., 2021). Through a one-step Zeta potential test, as shown in Fig. S4B, the Zeta potentials of BCDs and OxOPD are −26.07 and + 2.3 mV, respectively. This data shows that BCDs and OxOPD can form a donor-acceptor pair in FRET assembly through electrostatic interaction, and the distance is less than 10 nm. In addition, a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer was used to analyze the fluorescence quenching mechanism of BCDs. The fluorescence lifetime of BCDs exhibits a double exponential decay, and the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs/OxOPD is significantly lower than that of BCDs (Table S1). This phenomenon belongs to the dynamic quenching mechanism, which is further verified by the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs. Thus, the quenching mechanism is FRET rather than IFE. Therefore, in this design system, the quenching of fluorescent BCDs is caused by the FRET that produces OxOPD.
Several important parameters such as reaction time, temperature, pH, Ag+, and OPD concentration were tested to ensure the best experimental results.

The important parameters of AgNO3 concentration, OPD concentration, incubation temperature, pH, and reaction time were optimized by calculating the fluorescence intensity ratio between BCDs and OxOPD (I442/I562), and the best conditions were determined. As shown in Fig. S5A, as the Ag+ concentration increased from 0 to 90 µM, the I442/I562 value no longer changes, which means that the Ag+ and OPD react completely. As shown in Fig. S5B, when the OPD concentration exceeds 200 µM, the value of I442/I562 remains unchanged, which means that the system no longer produces OxOPD. As shown in Fig. S5C, BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH solution and BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH/ADA solution are at 25 ℃, Δ (I442/I562) is the largest at 30 °C and 35 °C. As shown in Fig. S5D, BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH solution and BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH/ADA solution are at pH= 5.5 Δ (I442/I562) is the largest. As shown in Fig. S5E, BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH solution and BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH/ADA solution remained unchanged after 20 min of reaction, indicating that the solution reaction was complete. Therefore, 90 µM of AgNO3, 200 µM of OPD, 25 °C, pH= 5.5, and 20 min were selected as the optimal experimental parameter for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal parameters, the ratio fluorescence responses of GSH were tested. As can be seen from Fig. 4A, the fluorescence emission peak of OxOPD at 562 nm decreases with the increase of GSH at the concentration of 0–320 µM, and the fluorescence emission peak of BCDs gradually increases. with the increase of GSH content, the fluorescence color gradually changed from orange to blue (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C shows a good linearity between I442/I562 and GSH concentration is within 0.1–200 µM, I442/I562 = 0.0303 CGSH+ 0.0752 (R2 =0.9967). Based on the fluorescence spectrum data, the International Commission on illumination (CIE) 1931 chromaticity diagram of GSH titration (Fig. 4D) was produced. When GSH concentration increases, the fluorescence color changed from the orange area turns into the blue area. This is the same as the color change transition seen in fluorescence visualization. Therefore, the ratio fluorescence method based on BCDs/Ag+/OPD is feasible to determine GSH. Based on the above research, through the fluorescence sensing system at 0.1 μM-200 μM GSH was detected within a certain concentration range of M, and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated as 0.07 µM (S/N = 3). Besides, as shown in Table 1, this method has superior performance compared with previously reported methods or smartphone programs in the previous literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ADA in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal parameters, the ratio fluorescence response of ADA was detected. Fig. 5A shows the fluorescence emission peak of OxOPD at 562 nm increases with the increase of ADA at the concentration of 0–300 µM, and the fluorescence emission intensity of BCDs gradually decreases. Fig. 5B shows that the fluorescence visualization color changes from blue to orange with the increase of ADA concentration. Thus, a good linear relationship between the value of I442/I562 and ADA at the concentration of 0.5–160 µM was presented (Fig. 5C). Based on the I442/I562 = −0.0333 CADA+ 5.5207 (R2 =0.9945), the LOD is 0.09 µM. Besides, the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram of ADA titration (Fig. 5D) is produced based on the fluorescence spectrum data. With the addition of ADA, the fluorescence color changes from blue to orange. This is the same as the color change transition seen in fluorescence visualization. Therefore, the ratio fluorescence method based on BCDs/ Ag+/OPD/GSH is feasible to determine ADA. In addition, we compared this method with other reported methods and the result was listed in Table 2. The detection limit or detection range of this method is better than other methods reported in the literature. Although the colorimetric method had high sensitivity, the linear range was narrow. However, there are few reports of using mobile phone programs to detect ADA content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, anti-interference, repeatability, and reproducibility study
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the selectivity of the BCDs/Ag+/OPD system, it was compared with common interfering substances. It includes two biological thiols, cysteine, and homocysteine, as well as other common disruptors in serum, including, KCl, ZnCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, CuCl2, Fe(NO3)3, NH4Cl, histidine, tryptophan, phenylalanine, tyrosine, leucine, alanine, hyaluronic acid, and homocysteine. The concentration of GSH was 200 μM, and the concentration of other interfering substances was 20 mM, which is 100 fold of GSH concentration. As shown in Fig. S6, after GSH and other interfering substances were added into BCDs/Ag+/OPD system, respectively, the I442/I562 values of each solution were determined by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The I442/I562 values in the solution with GSH increased sharply, while the I442/I562 values in the solution with other interfering substances did not change significantly. The I442/I562 values of GSH in the BCDs/Ag+/OPD system are close to those of GSH and other interfering substances in the Ag+/BCDs/OPD system. Therefore, the experimental results show that this system has high selectivity for GSH. Such high selectivity was due to the selective inhibition effect of GSH to the Ag+/OPD based autocatalytic reaction (Li et al., 2018). The selective inhibition effect of GSH was a comprehensive effect attributed to the high chelate ability of GSH to Ag+ (Splittgerber and Tappel, 1979; Tang et al., 2013), combining the high reduction activity of GSH (Ni et al., 2015).

Besides, the selective and anti-interference detection of ADA was also studied by using similar methods. Common interference substances in flour KCl, ZnCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, PbCl2, HgCl2, CdCl2, galactose, glucose, lactose, maltose, cysteine, homocysteine, histidine, tryptophan, Phenylalanine, Melamine, and Sudan III. The concentration of ADA is 160 µM, and the concentration of other interfering substances is 16 mM. As shown in Fig. S7, after ADA and other interfering substances were added to the BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH system, the I442/I562 values of each solution were measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The I442/I562 value in the solution with ADA was a sharp Increase, but the solution with other interfering substances has no obvious change. ADA was added to BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH system compared with ADA and each interfering substance mixed into BCDs/Ag+/OPD/GSH system, the values of I442/I562 in each solution were close. Therefore, the system has a high selectivity to ADA based on experimental phenomena.

The repeatability of the prepared sensor is highly concerned and of critical importance to realize routine and low-cost monitoring, which was investigated by repetitively determining 20 µM GSH and 20 µM ADA (Fig. S8). Acceptable RSDs of 0.67% in GSH and 1.06% in ADA were observed, respectively (n = 6). The reproducibility of the sensor including batch-to-batch reproducibility of the fabrication procedure was also investigated. Each sensor exhibited similar responses and the RSDs of 0.71% for GSH and 2.45% for ADA (n = 6) were obtained, indicating an excellent reproducibility of the prepared sensor. Obviously, these attractive characteristics make the prepared sensor have great potential in practical sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targets detection of GSH and ADA based on deep learning algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: In order to quickly extract color information from captured fluorescence images by the smartphone, we designed an applet of WeChat named "intelligent fluorescence analysis". This applet of WeChat uses yolov3 target detection based on deep learning. The statistical model created by convolution neural networks, deep learning, and simple image processing can achieve high accuracy. In this study, the yolov3 algorithm was used to classify and detect the test tube images, and then combined with an ordinary image processing algorithm to segment and extract RGB and HSV (Kuznetsova et al., 2020). To facilitate in-depth learning, the experimental group collected a total of 111 fluorescent images and then divided them into 60% of the training set and 40% of the test set. The images are composed of different fluorescent colors of different tubes, different angles of a single object, or multiple objects. As shown in Fig. S9. Yolov3 algorithm first labels the original images and then adjusts the image size to 416 * 416. The training data set was labeled according to voc2007 format. Then the trained yolov3 network was used for prediction, and the image annotation tool was used to label all the basic truth boundary boxes. Then the predicted image is segmented to extract the features of the segmented region of interest. As shown in Fig. S10, the backbone network of yolov3, darknet-53, has 53 convolution layers. Yolov3 has three prediction feature layers, and each prediction feature layer uses three scales. Nine groups of scales (10) are obtained by K-means clustering algorithm (10 × 13), (16 × 30)， (33 × 23), (30 × 61), (62 × 45), (59 × 119)， (116 × 90), (156 × 198), (373 × 326), and then three groups of scales on each prediction feature layer are obtained. Because the first predicted characteristic layer is 13 × 13, so larger targets will be predicted on it, and the second predicted feature layer size is 26 × 26, so the target with medium-scale will be predicted, and the third prediction feature layer has the highest fineness because the size is 52 × 52, so small targets will be predicted on it.

we use the false-negative rate (FNR), false-positive rate (FPR), precision, recall, and F1 score as the evaluation parameters to verify the effectiveness of the experiment on the trained yolov3 model (Lawal, 2021). The specific formula is as follows:
(1)
Precision =
TP
TP + FP
(2)
Recall =
TP
TP + FN
(3)
FPR = 1 - Precision =
FP
TP + FP
(4)
FNR = 1 - Recall =
FN
TP + FN
(5)
F1 =
2 * Precision * Recall
Precision + Recall

TP (true positive) represents the real test tube detected by the algorithm in the image, FP (false positive) represents the object mistaken for the test tube, and FN (false negative) represents the undetected test tube. respectively. Precision indicates the number of correct tests for all tubes, while recall gives the number of correct detections out of total ground-truth tubes. F1 score was conducted as a trade-off between Recall and Precision to represent the comprehensive performance of the trained models. The following results are obtained by calculating each evaluation index according to the appealing formula, the precision is 92.5%, the recall rate is 100.0%, the FPR is 7.5%, FNR is 0 and F1 is 96.1%. We provide all data and machine learning models, which can be downloaded and used by clicking the link below (link: https://pan.baidu.com/s/1bt0BkdigTpfQ5Qz_4av1BA. Extraction code: 37lv).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GSH and ADA detection based on smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence photometric analysis usually requires instruments such as a fluorescence spectrophotometer, which is limited to laboratory operation. Therefore, to realize the rapid detection on site, We have developed a WeChat applet based on smartphone. The WeChat applet uses the yolov3 algorithm to classify and detect test tube images, and then combines with ordinary image processing algorithms to segment and extract RGB and HSV. When a certain amount of GSH and ADA are added to the BCDs/Ag+/OPD system, fluorescence images were captured by the smartphone, then use the WeChat applet developed by us to process the image, and then The correlation between RGB and HSV values and glutathione and ADA concentrations was calculated. Linear analysis of GSH and ADA concentrations using RGB_R/G and RGB_R/B respectively. As shown in Fig. 6A. Use our WeChat applet to analyze, and the linear relationship between R/G and GSH concentration was consistent in the linear range of 0.1–200 µM, which decreases with the increase of concentration, the linear equation is R/G = −0.0052 CGSH + 1.0557, R2 = 0.9942. In the linear range of 0.5–160 µM, R/B and ADA concentration conform to the linear relationship, the linear equation is R/B= 0.0068 CADA-0.0082, R2 = 0.9926. To verify the accuracy of the linear equation obtained by the self-developed WeChat applet, we import the RGB and HSV values obtained by the applet into the origin software for analysis. Fig. 6B and Fig. 6C show that there is no significant difference between the two linear equations. Here, the parameters with the best linear relationship are used for demonstration, and the other parameters are placed in the Supporting Information. As shown in Fig. S11 and Fig. S12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH and ADA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the accuracy of smartphone sensing platform, we calculate the recovery of samples by standard addition method to evaluate the accuracy of this method. Firstly, we measured the initial contents of GSH and ADA in the sample by HPLC, then added different concentrations of GSH and ADA into the sample by standard addition method, took photos by smartphone (Fig. S13), and read out the target concentration through a self-developed applet. The recoveries of analysis were 97.48–102.84% for GSH and 99.59–104.88% for ADA (Table S2). Therefore, BCDs/ Ag+/OPD system can be used for the field analysis and determination of GSH and ADA in actual samples.

In summary, a portable smartphone-based ratio fluorescence nanoprobe optical sensing platform (BCDs/Ag+/OPD) was developed using machine learning technology for GSH and ADA detection. The sensing system has good sensitivity in the concentration range of 0.1–200 μM with the LOD of 0.07 μM for the detection of GSH and in the concentration range of 0.5–160 μM with the LOD of 0.09 μM, respectively. In addition, a portable detection platform based on the smartphone was developed for online analysis of GSH and ADA. The applet of WeChat uses the target detection of yolov3 of deep learning. The statistical model created by convolution neural networks, deep learning, and simple image processing can achieve high accuracy. The WeChat- applet successfully detected GSH in serum and ADA in flour, respectively. The results show that the deep learning-assisted smartphone-based sensing platform is highly accurate and convenient. Therefore, the method has high sensitivity and can detect low concentration GSH and ADA. Ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobes (RFNPs) have shown great potential in optical sensing applications. Machine learning has recently been successfully used to identify patterns and trends in data. This portable sensing platform based on machine learning and smartphones can be developed into a home portable detection platform, which is conducive to the early detection of disease diagnosis through the abnormality of glutathione. In addition, it can also be used as an alternative method to quickly and conveniently detect ADA in flour to ensure food safety.
